studyContent.title: The Day the River Spoke (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A story about Jahnavi, a girl in a coastal village who has a dream, in which nature, like a river, seems to speak to her.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a story about a bright young girl named Jahnavi who lives with her parents and three brothers in a coastal village in India, where her parents work on the farm. Jahnavi has a big dream. In the story, the world of nature around her, including a river, seems to come alive and speak to her, with sounds that splash, slither and murmur. Through this magical experience and her own determination, Jahnavi learns about following her dream and listening closely to the world around her. The story encourages us to have dreams, to work to make them come true, and to pay attention to nature.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Jahnavi, a girl in a coastal village, has a dream. In the story, nature and a river seem to speak to her, teaching her to follow her dream and listen to the world.

She has a dream: Jahnavi has a big dream she wants to make come true.
Nature speaks: In the story, the river and nature seem to come alive and speak to her.
The lesson: She learns to follow her dream and to listen closely to the world around her.

studyContent.title: Try Again (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A poem about King Bruce of Scotland who learns the value of perseverance by watching a spider try again and again.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This well-loved poem tells the story of King Bruce of Scotland, who is feeling sad and discouraged because he has tried again and again to do a great deed for his people but has failed. As he sits in a lonely mood, he watches a little spider trying to climb up to its web. The spider falls many times but keeps trying, and at last it succeeds. Watching this, King Bruce takes heart and decides to try once more himself. The poem teaches the important lesson that we should never give up: if at first we do not succeed, we must 'try, try again'.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: King Bruce of Scotland is sad after failing many times. Watching a spider keep trying until it succeeds, he learns never to give up: try, try again.
stepByStep: King Bruce is sad: King Bruce has failed many times in a great task and feels discouraged.
He watches a spider: He sees a little spider trying to climb up to its web.
The spider succeeds: The spider falls again and again but keeps trying until it succeeds.
The lesson: King Bruce learns to try again; we must never give up.

studyContent.title: Three Days to See (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An essay by Helen Keller, who was blind and deaf, about how precious our senses are and what she would wish to see in three days.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a moving essay by Helen Keller, who could neither see nor hear, yet led a remarkable life. In it, she explains how she finds hundreds of things to interest her through touch alone, feeling the delicate shape of a leaf and the smoothness of objects. She imagines what she would do if she were given just three days to see, describing the people, nature and beautiful things she would want to look at. Through this, she reminds those who can see and hear to value their senses and not take them for granted. The essay teaches us to appreciate the precious gifts of sight and our other senses, and to truly notice the beauty of the world.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Helen Keller, who was blind and deaf, writes about appreciating our senses. She imagines what she would see in three days, urging us to value sight and not take it for granted.
stepByStep: Helen Keller's world: Helen Keller, who could not see or hear, found joy in the world through touch.
Three days to see: She imagines what she would look at if given just three days of sight.
What she would see: She describes the people, nature and beauty she would want to see.
The lesson: She urges those who can see and hear to value their senses and not take them for granted.

studyContent.title: Animals, Birds, and Dr. Dolittle (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A story about Dr. Dolittle, a doctor who learns to talk to animals and understand their language.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a delightful story about Dr. Dolittle, a kind doctor who loves animals so much that he learns to talk to them and understand their language. By listening closely and observing their sounds and body language, he is able to communicate with all kinds of animals and birds, who become his friends and patients. The story is full of wit and humour and shows the special bond between humans and animals. It reminds us that if we observe animals carefully, we can understand a lot about what they are feeling and trying to tell us, and it encourages kindness towards all creatures.

studyContent.title: A Funny Man (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A humorous nonsense poem about a funny man who does everything in a silly, topsy-turvy way.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a light, humorous poem that describes a funny man the speaker meets walking down the street. The man does everything in a silly, mixed-up way: he wears a shoe upon his head and hats upon his feet, and behaves in odd but polite ways, such as offering a rose in a strange manner. The whole poem is a piece of nonsense humour, meant simply to make us laugh with its funny, topsy-turvy images. It shows how playful language and silly ideas can be great fun, and it celebrates the lighter, funnier side of life.

studyContent.title: Say the Right Thing (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A lesson about the importance of being polite and courteous, and saying the right thing to others, especially to guests.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This lesson teaches us the importance of politeness and courtesy in our words and behaviour. It draws on the Indian idea of 'Atithi Devo Bhava', which means 'the guest is like God', and shows why we should treat guests and others with respect and warmth. It gives examples of polite behaviour, like offering guests water, asking about their well-being, and seeing them to the door, and contrasts these with rude behaviour. The lesson also discusses how to disagree politely with friends without being rude. It reminds us that saying the right thing in the right way, with kindness and good manners, makes everyone feel respected and keeps our relationships pleasant.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: This lesson teaches politeness and courtesy, like the idea of 'Atithi Devo Bhava' (the guest is like God). Saying the right thing kindly makes everyone feel respected.
stepByStep: Politeness matters: The lesson teaches the importance of politeness and courtesy.
Atithi Devo Bhava: It draws on the idea that 'the guest is like God', so guests are treated with warmth.
Polite behaviour: Examples include offering water, asking about well-being and seeing guests to the door.
Disagreeing politely: It also shows how to disagree with friends politely, without being rude.

keywords.example: 'Atithi Devo Bhava' teaches us to honour guests.

studyContent.title: My Brother's Great Invention (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A story about a young inventor and his invention, celebrating creativity, curiosity and the spirit of discovery.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a light and entertaining story about a brother who tries to create a 'great invention'. Through his attempts, with their successes and funny failures, the story celebrates the spirit of creativity, curiosity and inventiveness. It reminds us that inventors need qualities like imagination, patience and the willingness to keep trying. The lesson also points to great Indian contributions to the world, such as the concept of zero invented by ancient Indian mathematicians and the practice of yoga. It encourages young readers to be curious, to dream up new ideas, and not to fear failure, because every invention and discovery begins with an idea and the courage to try.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A story about a brother trying to make a great invention. It celebrates creativity, curiosity and not fearing failure, and mentions Indian inventions like zero and yoga.

studyContent.title: Paper Boats (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A poem by Rabindranath Tagore in which a child floats paper boats down a stream, hoping they reach someone far away.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a beautiful and imaginative poem by Rabindranath Tagore, spoken by a child. Every day the child floats little paper boats, one by one, down a running stream, writing his name and the name of his village on them in big black letters. He hopes that someone in some far-off land will find his boats and know who he is. He loads his boats with flowers and dreams that they will be carried safely to the shore in the night. The poem beautifully captures a child's imagination, innocence and hope, and the wish to connect with the wider world. It celebrates the dreams and gentle longings of childhood.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: In this Tagore poem, a child floats paper boats down a stream, hoping someone far away will find them. It captures a child's imagination, innocence and hope.

studyContent.title: North, South, East, West (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An informative lesson about the different regions, states and rich diversity of India.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This lesson takes us on a journey across India, exploring its different regions, the north, south, east, west, north-east and centre, and the many states and union territories in each. It encourages students to learn about their own state and others, including their capitals, languages, rivers, foods and tourist attractions. Through this, the lesson shows the great geographical and cultural diversity of India, where each region has its own special features yet all are part of one country. It helps us appreciate how varied and beautiful India is and develops our knowledge of the geography and culture of our nation.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: This lesson explores India's regions, states and union territories from north to south, showing the country's great geographical and cultural diversity.

studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a lively poem by Robert Louis Stevenson, spoken by a child who longs to travel to exciting, faraway places. In his imagination, the child dreams of distant lands, where there are apes and parrots, hot deserts, great cities, and many wonderful and exotic sights. He imagines all the amazing places he would like to visit one day. The poem beautifully captures a child's sense of wonder, curiosity and longing to explore the wide world. It celebrates the joy of imagination and the dream of travel and adventure that many of us feel.

studyContent.title: Conquering the Summit (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An inspiring true story of Arunima Sinha, who, despite losing a leg, became the first female amputee to climb Mount Everest.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is an inspiring true story about Arunima Sinha and her remarkable journey of will, courage, determination and dedication. After a tragic accident in which she lost a leg, Arunima refused to give up on her dreams. With great determination and hard work, she trained hard and went on to become the first female amputee in the world to climb Mount Everest, the highest mountain on Earth. The story describes the qualities needed by a mountaineer, courage, fitness, determination and never giving up, and shows how Arunima overcame enormous difficulties. Her story is a powerful example of how a strong will can help a person conquer any challenge.

studyContent.title: Integers and Their Representation
studyContent.intro: Integers are the collection of whole numbers and their negatives, shown on a number line.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Integers are the set of numbers that includes all the whole numbers (0, 1, 2, 3, ...) together with the negative numbers (-1, -2, -3, ...). So integers are ..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, .... We can show integers on a number line, with zero in the middle, positive integers to the right and negative integers to the left. On the number line, a number is greater if it lies to the right, so 3 is greater than -5. Integers are used in daily life to show things like temperatures below zero, money owed, and depths below sea level.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Integers are whole numbers plus their negatives: ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, .... On a number line, numbers to the right are greater.
stepByStep: What integers are: Integers include all whole numbers and their negatives.
On a number line: Integers are shown on a number line with 0 in the middle.
Positives and negatives: Positive integers are to the right of 0, negative integers to the left.
Comparing integers: A number to the right is greater, so 3 > -5.

studyContent.title: Addition and Subtraction of Integers
studyContent.intro: Integers are added and subtracted using rules of signs, often pictured on a number line.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To add and subtract integers we use rules about their signs. To add two integers with the same sign, we add their values and keep the sign (for example, -3 + -4 = -7). To add two integers with different signs, we subtract the smaller value from the larger and take the sign of the larger (for example, -7 + 5 = -2). To subtract an integer, we add its opposite (additive inverse); for example, 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8. Addition of integers is commutative and associative, and zero is the additive identity. These rules let us calculate confidently with positive and negative numbers.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To add integers: same signs add and keep the sign; different signs subtract and take the larger's sign. To subtract, add the opposite.
stepByStep: Same signs: Add the values and keep the common sign, like -3 + -4 = -7.
Different signs: Subtract the smaller value from the larger and take the larger's sign, like -7 + 5 = -2.
Subtracting: To subtract an integer, add its opposite, like 5 - (-3) = 8.
Properties: Addition is commutative and associative; zero is the additive identity.

keywords.meaning: The opposite of a number, which adds to give zero.

studyContent.title: Multiplication of Integers
studyContent.intro: When multiplying integers, two like signs give a positive product and two unlike signs give a negative product.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: When we multiply integers, the sign of the answer depends on the signs of the numbers. The product of two positive integers is positive, and the product of two negative integers is also positive (like signs give a positive). The product of a positive and a negative integer is negative (unlike signs give a negative). For example, (-4) x (-3) = 12, but (-4) x 3 = -12. To find the product, we multiply the values and then apply the sign rule. Multiplication of integers is commutative, associative, and 1 is the multiplicative identity; multiplying by 0 gives 0.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Multiplying integers: like signs (++ or --) give a positive answer; unlike signs (+ and -) give a negative answer. So (-4) x (-3) = 12.
stepByStep: Like signs: The product of two positives or two negatives is positive.
Unlike signs: The product of a positive and a negative is negative.
Find the product: Multiply the values, then apply the sign rule.
Properties: Multiplication is commutative, associative; 1 is the identity and multiplying by 0 gives 0.

keywords.meaning: The result of multiplying.
Signs that are the same.
The number (1) that does not change a product.

studyContent.title: Division of Integers
studyContent.intro: When dividing integers, like signs give a positive quotient and unlike signs give a negative quotient.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Division of integers follows the same sign rules as multiplication. When we divide two integers with like signs (both positive or both negative), the quotient is positive; when we divide two integers with unlike signs, the quotient is negative. For example, (-12) ÷ (-3) = 4, but (-12) ÷ 3 = -4. To divide, we divide the values and apply the sign rule. Note that we cannot divide any number by zero. Understanding division of integers lets us solve a wider range of problems involving positive and negative numbers.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Dividing integers: like signs give a positive answer; unlike signs give a negative answer. So (-12) ÷ (-3) = 4. We cannot divide by zero.
stepByStep: Like signs: Dividing two integers with the same sign gives a positive quotient.
Unlike signs: Dividing two integers with different signs gives a negative quotient.
Find the quotient: Divide the values, then apply the sign rule.
No division by zero: We cannot divide any number by zero.

studyContent.title: Multiplication of Fractions
studyContent.intro: Fractions are multiplied by multiplying the numerators together and the denominators together.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply two fractions, we multiply the numerators (top numbers) together to get the new numerator, and multiply the denominators (bottom numbers) together to get the new denominator, then simplify. For example, (2/3) x (4/5) = 8/15. To multiply a fraction by a whole number, we multiply the whole number with the numerator. The word 'of' in problems means multiplication, so '1/2 of 10' means (1/2) x 10 = 5. When we multiply a proper fraction by another proper fraction, the product is smaller than both, which is a special feature of fractions less than 1.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To multiply fractions, multiply the tops together and the bottoms together, then simplify. So (2/3) x (4/5) = 8/15. 'Of' means multiply.
stepByStep: Multiply numerators: Multiply the top numbers to get the new numerator.
Multiply denominators: Multiply the bottom numbers to get the new denominator.
Simplify: Reduce the answer to its simplest form, like 8/15.
'Of' means multiply: '1/2 of 10' means (1/2) x 10 = 5.

studyContent.title: Division of Fractions
studyContent.intro: To divide by a fraction, we multiply by its reciprocal (the fraction turned upside down).
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Dividing by a fraction is done by multiplying by its reciprocal. The reciprocal of a fraction is found by interchanging its numerator and denominator; for example, the reciprocal of 3/4 is 4/3. To divide one fraction by another, we keep the first fraction, change the division sign to multiplication, and use the reciprocal of the second fraction. For example, (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 10/12 = 5/6. The same method works to divide a whole number by a fraction or a fraction by a whole number. This makes division of fractions simple once we know the reciprocal.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To divide by a fraction, flip it (its reciprocal) and multiply. So (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 5/6.
stepByStep: Find the reciprocal: The reciprocal of a fraction interchanges its numerator and denominator, like 3/4 to 4/3.
Change to multiplication: Keep the first fraction and change ÷ to x.
Multiply by the reciprocal: Multiply by the reciprocal of the second fraction.
Simplify: Simplify the answer, like (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = 5/6.

keywords.meaning: A fraction turned upside down.

Reduce a fraction fully.

studyContent.title: Multiplication of Decimals
studyContent.intro: Decimals are multiplied like whole numbers, then the decimal point is placed by counting decimal places.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply decimal numbers, we first multiply them as if they were whole numbers, ignoring the decimal points. Then we count the total number of digits after the decimal points in both numbers, and place the decimal point in the answer so that it has that many decimal places. For example, 2.5 x 1.2: multiplying 25 x 12 = 300, and there are two decimal places in total, so the answer is 3.00 = 3. To multiply a decimal by 10, 100 or 1000, we simply move the decimal point one, two or three places to the right. These rules make decimal multiplication straightforward.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Multiply decimals like whole numbers, then count the total decimal places and place the point. Multiplying by 10, 100, 1000 moves the point right.
stepByStep: Multiply as whole numbers: Ignore the decimal points and multiply as whole numbers.
Count decimal places: Count the total digits after the decimal points in both numbers.
Place the point: Put the decimal point in the answer with that many decimal places.
By 10, 100, 1000: Move the decimal point one, two or three places to the right.

studyContent.title: Division of Decimals
studyContent.intro: Decimals are divided by adjusting the decimal point, especially by making the divisor a whole number.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To divide decimals, we handle the decimal point carefully. To divide a decimal by 10, 100 or 1000, we move the decimal point one, two or three places to the left; for example, 4.5 ÷ 10 = 0.45. To divide a decimal by a whole number, we divide as usual and place the decimal point in the quotient directly above its place in the dividend. To divide a decimal by another decimal, we first make the divisor a whole number by multiplying both numbers by 10, 100 or 1000, and then divide. These methods let us divide decimal numbers accurately.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To divide decimals: by 10, 100, 1000 move the point left; by a whole number, divide normally; by a decimal, make the divisor a whole number first.
stepByStep: By 10, 100, 1000: Move the decimal point one, two or three places to the left, like 4.5 ÷ 10 = 0.45.
By a whole number: Divide as usual and place the point above its place in the dividend.
By a decimal: Make the divisor a whole number by multiplying both by 10, 100 or 1000.
Then divide: Divide the adjusted numbers to get the answer.

The number we divide by.

studyContent.title: Collecting and Organising Data
studyContent.intro: Data is information collected for a purpose, and it is organised in tables to study it easily.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Data is a collection of numbers or information gathered for a particular purpose, such as the marks of students or the runs scored in matches. Raw data collected as it comes is hard to read, so we organise it. We can arrange data in order, and use a frequency distribution table with tally marks to show how many times each value occurs (its frequency). The smallest and largest values give us the range of the data. Organising data well is the first step to understanding it and drawing useful conclusions.

studyContent.title: Mean, Median and Mode
studyContent.intro: Mean, median and mode are three measures that represent the central or typical value of data.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To describe a set of data with a single representative value, we use measures of central tendency. The mean (arithmetic average) is found by adding all the values and dividing by the number of values. The median is the middle value when the data is arranged in order; if there are two middle values, the median is their average. The mode is the value that occurs most often in the data. Each measure tells us something about the 'typical' value, and which one to use depends on the situation. These measures help us summarise and compare data.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Mean is the average (sum ÷ count), median is the middle value in order, and mode is the most frequent value. They show the typical value of data.
stepByStep: Mean: The average: add all values and divide by the number of values.
Median: The middle value when the data is arranged in order.
Mode: The value that occurs most often in the data.
Their use: Each gives a typical value; which to use depends on the situation.

studyContent.title: Bar Graphs
studyContent.intro: A bar graph shows data using bars, making it easy to compare quantities at a glance.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A bar graph represents data using rectangular bars of equal width, where the length or height of each bar shows the value it represents. The bars are drawn with equal gaps on a graph with two axes and a suitable scale. Bar graphs make it easy to compare different quantities just by looking at the bars. A double bar graph uses two bars side by side for each item, which is useful for comparing two sets of data at once, such as the marks of two students in different subjects. Bar graphs present data clearly and are widely used.

studyContent.title: Chance and Probability
studyContent.intro: Probability tells us how likely an event is to happen, on a scale from impossible to certain.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: In daily life some events are certain, some are impossible, and many are uncertain. Probability is a way of measuring how likely an uncertain event is to happen. For an experiment with equally likely outcomes, the probability of an event is the number of favourable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. For example, when tossing a coin, there are two outcomes (head or tail), so the probability of getting a head is 1/2. Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Understanding chance helps us reason about games, weather and many real-life situations.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Probability measures how likely an event is, from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Tossing a coin, the chance of a head is 1/2.
stepByStep: Events differ in chance: Some events are certain, some impossible, and many uncertain.
What probability is: Probability measures how likely an uncertain event is to happen.
Calculate it: Probability = favourable outcomes / total outcomes for equally likely outcomes.
The scale: Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

keywords.example: The probability of a head is 1/2.

studyContent.title: Setting Up Equations
studyContent.intro: An equation is a statement that two expressions are equal, often containing an unknown called a variable.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: An equation is a mathematical statement that says two expressions are equal, shown by an equals sign. It usually contains a variable, an unknown quantity represented by a letter like x. For example, x + 3 = 8 is an equation. We can set up an equation from a word problem by representing the unknown with a variable and writing the given condition as an equation. The value of the variable that makes the two sides equal is called the solution of the equation. Equations are powerful tools for solving real-life problems where some quantity is unknown.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: An equation says two expressions are equal, like x + 3 = 8, with a variable (unknown) such as x. We can write equations from word problems.

The variable: It contains a variable, an unknown shown by a letter like x.

studyContent.title: Solving Equations
studyContent.intro: An equation is solved by doing the same operation on both sides to find the value of the variable.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To solve an equation means to find the value of the variable that makes it true. We do this by keeping the equation balanced: whatever operation we do to one side, we must do the same to the other side. We can add, subtract, multiply or divide both sides by the same number to gradually get the variable alone on one side. For example, to solve x + 3 = 8, we subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5. Another method is transposing, where we move a term to the other side and change its sign. We can check the solution by putting it back into the equation.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To solve an equation, do the same operation to both sides to get the variable alone. For x + 3 = 8, subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5.
stepByStep: Keep it balanced: Whatever you do to one side, do the same to the other side.
Isolate the variable: Add, subtract, multiply or divide both sides to get the variable alone.
Example: For x + 3 = 8, subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5.
Check the answer: Put the value back into the equation to check it is correct.

keywords.example: We solve x + 3 = 8 to get x = 5.

studyContent.title: Applications of Simple Equations
studyContent.intro: Simple equations are used to solve real-life word problems involving an unknown quantity.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Simple equations are very useful for solving everyday problems where some quantity is unknown. To solve a word problem, we read it carefully, choose a variable for the unknown, write an equation from the given conditions, solve the equation, and then state the answer in words. For example, 'Three times a number plus 4 is 19' becomes 3x + 4 = 19, which gives x = 5. We should always check that our answer fits the original problem. This shows how algebra helps us turn real situations into equations and find the unknown.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: We use equations to solve word problems: pick a variable for the unknown, form an equation, solve it, and state the answer. '3 times a number + 4 is 19' gives x = 5.

studyContent.title: Related Angles
studyContent.intro: Pairs of angles can be related as complementary, supplementary, adjacent, linear pair or vertically opposite.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Some pairs of angles have special relationships. Two angles are complementary if their measures add up to 90 degrees, and supplementary if they add up to 180 degrees. Two angles are adjacent if they have a common vertex and a common arm but do not overlap. A linear pair is a pair of adjacent angles whose non-common arms form a straight line, so they add up to 180 degrees. Vertically opposite angles are formed when two lines cross, and they are always equal. Knowing these relationships helps us find unknown angles.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Angles can be complementary (add to 90), supplementary (add to 180), adjacent, a linear pair (add to 180), or vertically opposite (equal).
stepByStep: Complementary: Two angles that add up to 90 degrees.
Supplementary: Two angles that add up to 180 degrees.
Adjacent and linear pair: Adjacent angles share a vertex and arm; a linear pair adds to 180 degrees.
Vertically opposite: Angles opposite each other when two lines cross are always equal.

Two angles adding to 180 degrees.

keywords.example: Crossing lines make equal opposite angles.

studyContent.title: Pairs of Lines
studyContent.intro: Lines can be intersecting or parallel, and a transversal is a line that cuts two or more lines.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: When we study lines, we look at how they relate to each other. Two lines that cross each other at a point are called intersecting lines, and the point where they meet is the point of intersection. Two lines in the same plane that never meet, no matter how far they are extended, are called parallel lines. A transversal is a line that crosses two or more lines at different points. When a transversal cuts two lines, it forms several pairs of angles, such as corresponding angles, alternate angles and co-interior angles, which are important for studying parallel lines.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Lines that cross are intersecting; lines that never meet are parallel. A transversal is a line that cuts two or more lines, forming pairs of angles.
stepByStep: Intersecting lines: Two lines that cross at a point are intersecting lines.
Parallel lines: Two lines in a plane that never meet are parallel lines.
Transversal: A line that cuts two or more lines at different points.
Angle pairs: A transversal forms corresponding, alternate and co-interior angles.

studyContent.title: Angles made by a Transversal on Parallel Lines
studyContent.intro: When a transversal cuts parallel lines, corresponding and alternate angles are equal, and co-interior angles are supplementary.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: When a transversal crosses a pair of parallel lines, special angle relationships appear. Corresponding angles (in matching positions) are equal. Alternate interior angles (on opposite sides of the transversal between the lines) are equal, and alternate exterior angles are also equal. Co-interior angles (on the same side of the transversal between the lines) are supplementary, adding up to 180 degrees. These properties let us find unknown angles when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and they can also be used to check whether two lines are parallel.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: When a transversal cuts parallel lines: corresponding angles are equal, alternate angles are equal, and co-interior angles add to 180 degrees.
stepByStep: Set up: A transversal crosses a pair of parallel lines.
Corresponding angles: Angles in matching positions are equal.
Alternate angles: Alternate interior (and exterior) angles are equal.
Co-interior angles: Angles on the same side between the lines add up to 180 degrees.

studyContent.title: Medians and Altitudes of a Triangle
studyContent.intro: A median joins a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side, and an altitude is the perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A triangle has special line segments. A median of a triangle is a line segment that joins a vertex (corner) to the midpoint of the opposite side. Every triangle has three medians, and they all meet at a single point. An altitude of a triangle is the perpendicular line segment drawn from a vertex to the opposite side (or the line containing it). Every triangle has three altitudes. The altitude represents the 'height' of the triangle from a particular side. Knowing medians and altitudes helps us understand the structure of triangles and is useful in finding areas.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A median joins a corner to the middle of the opposite side. An altitude is the perpendicular from a corner to the opposite side. Each triangle has three of each.

Altitude: An altitude is the perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side.

studyContent.title: Angle Sum Property and Exterior Angle
studyContent.intro: The three angles of a triangle add up to 180 degrees, and an exterior angle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Two important properties relate to the angles of a triangle. The angle sum property states that the sum of the three interior angles of any triangle is always 180 degrees. So if two angles are known, the third can be found by subtracting their sum from 180 degrees. The exterior angle property states that an exterior angle of a triangle (formed by extending one side) is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles. These properties are very useful for finding unknown angles in triangles and in many geometry problems.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: The three angles of a triangle always add to 180 degrees. An exterior angle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles.
stepByStep: Angle sum property: The three interior angles of a triangle add up to 180 degrees.
Find the third angle: If two angles are known, subtract their sum from 180 to find the third.
Exterior angle: An exterior angle is formed by extending one side of the triangle.
Exterior angle property: An exterior angle equals the sum of the two interior opposite angles.

topicTitle: Types of Triangles and the Triangle Inequality

studyContent.title: Types of Triangles and the Triangle Inequality
studyContent.intro: Triangles are classified by their sides and angles, and the sum of any two sides is always greater than the third.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Triangles can be classified in two ways. By their sides: an equilateral triangle has all three sides equal, an isosceles triangle has two sides equal, and a scalene triangle has all sides different. By their angles: an acute triangle has all angles less than 90 degrees, a right triangle has one angle of 90 degrees, and an obtuse triangle has one angle more than 90 degrees. An important rule is the triangle inequality: the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the length of the third side. This rule tells us whether three given lengths can form a triangle.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Triangles are classified by sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and angles (acute, right, obtuse). Any two sides always add to more than the third.
stepByStep: By sides: Equilateral (all equal), isosceles (two equal) or scalene (all different).
By angles: Acute (all less than 90), right (one 90) or obtuse (one more than 90 degrees).
Triangle inequality: The sum of any two sides is always greater than the third side.
Forming a triangle: This rule tells whether three lengths can make a triangle.

keywords.meaning: A triangle with two sides equal.

studyContent.title: Pythagoras Theorem
studyContent.intro: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: The Pythagoras theorem is an important property of right-angled triangles (triangles with one 90-degree angle). The side opposite the right angle is the longest side, called the hypotenuse. The theorem states that the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. If the two shorter sides are a and b and the hypotenuse is c, then a-squared + b-squared = c-squared. For example, in a triangle with sides 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is 5 because 9 + 16 = 25. This theorem is used to find an unknown side and to check whether a triangle is right-angled.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: In a right triangle, (hypotenuse)^2 = (side1)^2 + (side2)^2. For sides 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is 5, since 9 + 16 = 25.
stepByStep: Right triangle: Pythagoras theorem applies to triangles with a 90-degree angle.
The hypotenuse: The longest side, opposite the right angle, is the hypotenuse.
The theorem: Square of the hypotenuse = sum of squares of the other two sides.
Use it: Find an unknown side or check if a triangle is right-angled.

The longest side, opposite the right angle.
keywords.example: The hypotenuse is the longest side.

keywords.example: A right triangle has one right angle.

studyContent.title: Congruence of Figures
studyContent.intro: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Two figures are said to be congruent if they are exactly the same in both shape and size, so that one can be placed exactly over the other and they cover each other completely. For example, two line segments are congruent if they have the same length, and two angles are congruent if they have the same measure. Congruent figures are like exact copies of each other. The symbol for congruence is a special equals sign with a curve on top. When two triangles are congruent, their corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal. Understanding congruence helps us compare shapes precisely.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size, so one fits exactly over the other. Congruent triangles have equal sides and angles.
stepByStep: What congruent means: Two figures are congruent if they have the same shape and size.
They fit exactly: One congruent figure can be placed exactly over the other.
Examples: Segments are congruent if equal in length; angles if equal in measure.
Congruent triangles: Their corresponding sides and angles are equal.

studyContent.title: Criteria for Congruence of Triangles
studyContent.intro: Triangles can be shown to be congruent using the SSS, SAS, ASA and RHS criteria.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: We do not need to check all six measurements (three sides and three angles) to prove two triangles are congruent; certain sets are enough. The SSS (Side-Side-Side) criterion: if the three sides of one triangle equal the three sides of another, they are congruent. The SAS (Side-Angle-Side) criterion: if two sides and the angle between them are equal, the triangles are congruent. The ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) criterion: if two angles and the side between them are equal, they are congruent. The RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) criterion applies to right triangles: if the hypotenuse and one side are equal, they are congruent. Note that AAA (all angles equal) does not prove congruence, only similar shape.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Triangles are congruent if they match by SSS (three sides), SAS (two sides and included angle), ASA (two angles and included side), or RHS (right triangles).
stepByStep: SSS: Three sides of one triangle equal to three sides of another make them congruent.
SAS: Two sides and the angle between them equal make triangles congruent.
ASA: Two angles and the side between them equal make triangles congruent.
RHS: For right triangles, equal hypotenuse and one side make them congruent (AAA does not work).

keywords.example: SSS proves congruence with three sides.

keywords.example: SAS uses two sides and the angle between.

studyContent.title: Ratio, Proportion and Percentage
studyContent.intro: A ratio compares two quantities, a proportion equates two ratios, and a percentage is a fraction out of 100.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: We often compare quantities. A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division, written as a : b. When two ratios are equal, they are said to be in proportion. A percentage is a special way of comparing a quantity to 100; 'per cent' means 'out of 100', written with the symbol %. We can convert between fractions, decimals and percentages: for example, 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%. To find a percentage of a quantity, we multiply; for example, 20% of 50 = (20/100) x 50 = 10. Percentages are widely used in marks, discounts and comparisons.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A ratio compares two quantities (a : b); equal ratios are in proportion; a percentage is 'out of 100'. So 1/2 = 50%, and 20% of 50 = 10.
stepByStep: Ratio: A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind, written a : b.
Proportion: When two ratios are equal, they are in proportion.
Percentage: A percentage compares a quantity to 100; % means 'out of 100'.
Convert and find: 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%; to find a percentage of a number, multiply, like 20% of 50 = 10.

studyContent.title: Profit and Loss
studyContent.intro: Profit or loss is found by comparing the cost price and selling price of an item, often as a percentage.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: When something is bought and sold, we compare its cost price (CP), the price at which it is bought, with its selling price (SP), the price at which it is sold. If the selling price is more than the cost price, there is a profit (gain); if it is less, there is a loss. Profit = SP - CP, and Loss = CP - SP. Profit or loss is often expressed as a percentage of the cost price: Profit% = (Profit / CP) x 100, and Loss% = (Loss / CP) x 100. These ideas are used in everyday buying and selling to know how much was gained or lost.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Profit happens when selling price is more than cost price; loss when it is less. Profit% and Loss% are found as a percentage of the cost price.
stepByStep: Cost and selling price: Cost price (CP) is the buying price; selling price (SP) is the selling price.
Profit or loss: If SP > CP there is profit; if SP < CP there is loss.
Calculate them: Profit = SP - CP; Loss = CP - SP.
As a percentage: Profit% = (Profit / CP) x 100; Loss% = (Loss / CP) x 100.

studyContent.title: Simple Interest
studyContent.intro: Simple interest is the extra money paid for borrowing or earned for lending, calculated on the principal.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: When we borrow money or deposit money in a bank, we pay or earn extra money called interest. The money borrowed or deposited is called the principal (P). The interest is usually charged at a certain rate per cent per year, called the rate (R), for a certain time in years (T). Simple interest is calculated using the formula: SI = (P x R x T) / 100. The total amount to be paid back is the Amount = Principal + Simple Interest. For example, the simple interest on 1000 rupees at 10% for 2 years is (1000 x 10 x 2)/100 = 200 rupees. Simple interest is used in everyday money matters.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Simple interest is the extra money on a loan or deposit: SI = (P x R x T)/100. The total to repay is Amount = Principal + Interest.
stepByStep: Interest and principal: Interest is extra money on borrowed/deposited money (the principal).
Rate and time: Interest depends on the rate (R, per cent per year) and time (T, in years).
The formula: Simple interest SI = (P x R x T) / 100.
The amount: Amount = Principal + Simple Interest.

studyContent.title: Rational Numbers and Their Representation
studyContent.intro: A rational number is a number that can be written as a fraction p/q, where q is not zero.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A rational number is any number that can be written in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q is not zero. This includes all integers, fractions and their negatives; for example, 3, -2, 1/2 and -3/4 are all rational numbers. A rational number is positive if its numerator and denominator have the same sign, and negative if they have different signs. We can show rational numbers on a number line, just like integers and fractions. A rational number is in its standard form when the denominator is positive and the numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A rational number can be written as p/q (q not zero), like 1/2, -3/4 or 3. They can be positive or negative and shown on a number line.
stepByStep: What it is: A rational number can be written as p/q, where p and q are integers and q is not zero.
Many forms: Integers, fractions and their negatives are all rational numbers.
Positive or negative: It is positive if p and q have the same sign, negative if different.
Standard form: In standard form, the denominator is positive with no common factor with the numerator.

studyContent.title: Comparing Rational Numbers
studyContent.intro: Rational numbers are compared by writing them with the same denominator or by using the number line.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: To compare two rational numbers and find which is greater, we can write them with the same positive denominator and then compare the numerators; the one with the larger numerator is greater. We can also compare them using a number line, where a number to the right is greater. Every positive rational number is greater than zero, and every negative rational number is less than zero, so positive numbers are always greater than negative ones. There are also infinitely many rational numbers between any two given rational numbers. Comparing rational numbers lets us order them.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To compare rational numbers, give them the same denominator and compare numerators, or use a number line. Positive numbers are always greater than negatives.
stepByStep: Same denominator: Write the rational numbers with the same positive denominator.
Compare numerators: The one with the larger numerator is greater.
Number line: On a number line, a number to the right is greater.
Positive vs negative: Positive rationals are always greater than negative ones.

studyContent.title: Operations on Rational Numbers
studyContent.intro: Rational numbers can be added, subtracted, multiplied and divided using rules like those for fractions.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: We can do all four operations with rational numbers. To add or subtract rational numbers, we write them with the same denominator and then add or subtract the numerators. To multiply two rational numbers, we multiply the numerators together and the denominators together. To divide by a rational number, we multiply by its reciprocal. The sign rules for integers also apply. For example, (2/3) + (1/3) = 1, (2/3) x (3/4) = 1/2, and (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 5/6. These operations let us calculate with all rational numbers, positive and negative.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Add/subtract rational numbers with a common denominator; multiply by multiplying tops and bottoms; divide by multiplying by the reciprocal. Sign rules apply.
stepByStep: Add and subtract: Use a common denominator, then add or subtract the numerators.
Multiply: Multiply the numerators together and the denominators together.
Divide: Multiply by the reciprocal of the divisor.
Sign rules: The sign rules for integers also apply to rational numbers.

studyContent.title: Construction of Parallel Lines
studyContent.intro: Using a ruler and compass, we can construct a line parallel to a given line through a point not on it.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Practical geometry is about drawing accurate figures using a ruler and a compass. One important construction is drawing a line parallel to a given line through a point outside it. The method uses the property that when a transversal cuts two lines making equal alternate (or corresponding) angles, the lines are parallel. We draw a transversal from the point to the given line, copy the angle at the new point using a compass, and draw the parallel line. This construction relies on the angle properties of parallel lines and gives us an exact parallel line without measuring with a protractor.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Using a ruler and compass, we can draw a line parallel to a given line through an outside point, by copying equal alternate or corresponding angles.
stepByStep: Tools and goal: Using a ruler and compass, draw a parallel line through a point outside a given line.
Use the angle property: Equal alternate or corresponding angles mean two lines are parallel.
Copy the angle: Draw a transversal, then copy the angle at the new point with a compass.
Draw the parallel: The line drawn through the equal angle is parallel to the given line.

A tool for drawing arcs and copying angles.

studyContent.title: Construction of Triangles
studyContent.intro: Triangles can be constructed accurately when certain measurements are given, using SSS, SAS, ASA or RHS conditions.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: We can construct a triangle accurately using a ruler, compass and protractor when enough measurements are given. The conditions match the congruence criteria. With SSS, we are given the three sides. With SAS, we are given two sides and the angle between them. With ASA, we are given two angles and the side between them. With RHS, we construct a right triangle given the hypotenuse and one side. In each case, we draw the parts in order, using the compass to mark lengths and the protractor for angles, to complete the triangle. These constructions show how the congruence conditions also tell us when a triangle is uniquely determined.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Triangles can be constructed when given enough measurements: SSS (three sides), SAS (two sides and the angle between), ASA (two angles and the side between) or RHS.

SSS and SAS: Construct from three sides (SSS) or two sides and the included angle (SAS).
ASA and RHS: Construct from two angles and the included side (ASA) or a right triangle (RHS).
Draw step by step: Use ruler, compass and protractor to draw the parts in order.

studyContent.title: Perimeter and Area of Squares and Rectangles
studyContent.intro: Perimeter is the distance around a shape and area is the surface it covers; both have formulas for squares and rectangles.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: The perimeter of a closed figure is the total distance around its boundary, and the area is the amount of surface it covers. For a rectangle with length l and breadth b, the perimeter is 2 x (l + b) and the area is l x b. For a square with side s, the perimeter is 4 x s and the area is s x s. Perimeter is measured in units of length (like cm) and area in square units (like square cm). These formulas help us find the boundary length and surface of common shapes, useful for fencing, flooring and many real tasks.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Perimeter is the distance around a shape; area is the surface it covers. Rectangle: perimeter 2(l+b), area l x b. Square: perimeter 4s, area s x s.
stepByStep: Perimeter and area: Perimeter is the distance around; area is the surface covered.
Rectangle: Perimeter = 2 x (length + breadth); area = length x breadth.
Square: Perimeter = 4 x side; area = side x side.
Units: Perimeter is in length units; area is in square units.

studyContent.title: Area of Triangles and Parallelograms
studyContent.intro: The area of a parallelogram is base times height, and the area of a triangle is half of base times height.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: We can find the areas of triangles and parallelograms using simple formulas. The area of a parallelogram is equal to its base multiplied by its height (the perpendicular distance between the base and the opposite side): Area = base x height. A triangle is half of a parallelogram, so the area of a triangle is half of base times height: Area = (1/2) x base x height. In both, the height must be measured perpendicular to the base. These formulas let us find the areas of many shapes, since complex shapes can be split into triangles and parallelograms.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Area of a parallelogram = base x height. Area of a triangle = (1/2) x base x height. The height is measured perpendicular to the base.

Splitting shapes: Complex shapes can be split into triangles and parallelograms.

studyContent.title: Circumference and Area of a Circle
studyContent.intro: The circumference of a circle is the distance around it, and both circumference and area use the number pi.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A circle is a round closed figure, and the distance around it is called its circumference. The distance from the centre to any point on the circle is the radius (r), and the distance across through the centre is the diameter (d = 2r). The circumference of a circle is given by C = 2 x pi x r (or pi x d), where pi is a special number approximately equal to 22/7 or 3.14. The area of a circle is given by A = pi x r x r (pi times the radius squared). These formulas let us find the boundary and surface of circular objects, like wheels, plates and pipes.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A circle's circumference (distance around) is 2 x pi x r, and its area is pi x r x r, where pi is about 22/7 or 3.14, and r is the radius.
stepByStep: Parts of a circle: The radius is centre to edge; the diameter is across through the centre (d = 2r).
Circumference: The distance around a circle is C = 2 x pi x r (or pi x d).
The number pi: Pi is a special number, about 22/7 or 3.14.
Area: The area of a circle is A = pi x r x r.

studyContent.title: Terms, Factors and Coefficients
studyContent.intro: Algebraic expressions are made of terms, which are formed from factors, and the number part of a term is its coefficient.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression is formed using variables (letters) and constants (numbers) joined by operations. An expression is made up of terms, which are separated by plus or minus signs; for example, in 3x + 5, the terms are 3x and 5. Each term is a product of factors; in the term 3x, the factors are 3 and x. The numerical factor of a term is called its coefficient; in 3x, the coefficient of x is 3. Terms that have the same variables (like 3x and 5x) are called like terms, while terms with different variables are unlike terms. Understanding these parts is the basis of working with algebra.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Algebraic expressions are made of terms (like 3x and 5), each made of factors. The number in a term is its coefficient. Like terms have the same variables.
stepByStep: Expressions and terms: An expression is made of terms separated by + or - signs, like 3x and 5 in 3x + 5.
Factors: Each term is a product of factors; in 3x the factors are 3 and x.
Coefficient: The numerical factor of a term; in 3x the coefficient of x is 3.
Like and unlike terms: Like terms have the same variables (3x, 5x); unlike terms differ.

keywords.meaning: A part of an expression separated by + or -.
The numerical factor of a term.
Terms with the same variables.

studyContent.realLifeExample: In 3x + 5, the terms are 3x and 5, and the coefficient of x is 3.

topicTitle: Types of Expressions and Their Addition
sourceLabel: Based on NCERT Mathematics + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: Types of Expressions and Their Addition
studyContent.intro: Expressions are named by the number of terms, and like terms can be added or subtracted.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Algebraic expressions are named by how many terms they have: a monomial has one term (like 3x), a binomial has two terms (like 3x + 5), and a trinomial has three terms (like x squared + 2x + 1); expressions with one or more terms in general are called polynomials. We can add and subtract expressions by combining their like terms: we add or subtract the coefficients of like terms and keep the variable part the same. For example, 3x + 5x = 8x, and (2x + 3) + (4x + 1) = 6x + 4. Unlike terms cannot be combined. This lets us simplify and work with expressions.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Expressions are monomials (1 term), binomials (2 terms) or trinomials (3 terms). We add expressions by combining like terms, so 3x + 5x = 8x.
stepByStep: Types by terms: Monomial (1 term), binomial (2 terms), trinomial (3 terms).
Combine like terms: Add or subtract the coefficients of like terms, keeping the variable.
Example: 3x + 5x = 8x, and (2x + 3) + (4x + 1) = 6x + 4.
Unlike terms: Unlike terms cannot be combined.

An expression with two terms.
Terms with the same variables that can be combined.

studyContent.title: Finding the Value of an Expression
studyContent.intro: The value of an algebraic expression is found by substituting given numbers for the variables.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression has different values depending on the value of its variable. To find the value of an expression, we substitute (put in) the given number in place of the variable and then calculate. For example, the value of 3x + 5 when x = 2 is 3 x 2 + 5 = 11. This idea is used to make and use formulas, such as the formula for the perimeter of a square (4 x side) or the area of a rectangle (length x breadth), where we substitute the given measurements. Finding the value of expressions connects algebra to real calculations.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: To find an expression's value, put the given number in place of the variable and calculate. So 3x + 5 with x = 2 is 3 x 2 + 5 = 11.
stepByStep: Expressions have values: An expression's value depends on the value of its variable.
Substitute: Put the given number in place of the variable.
Calculate: Work out the result, like 3x + 5 with x = 2 gives 11.
Using formulas: Formulas like perimeter = 4 x side use substitution.

studyContent.title: Exponents
studyContent.intro: An exponent shows how many times a number (the base) is multiplied by itself.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Exponents give us a short way to write repeated multiplication. When a number is multiplied by itself several times, we write it using a base and an exponent (or power); for example, 2 x 2 x 2 = 2 cubed, written as 2 with a small 3, which equals 8. Here 2 is the base and 3 is the exponent, meaning 2 is used three times. Exponents help us write very large numbers in a short form. For example, 10 raised to the power 5 means 10 multiplied by itself five times, which is 1,00,000. Reading and writing numbers with exponents makes large calculations much easier.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: An exponent shows repeated multiplication: 2 cubed (2 to the power 3) means 2 x 2 x 2 = 8. The base is 2 and the exponent is 3.
stepByStep: Repeated multiplication: An exponent is a short way to write repeated multiplication.
Base and exponent: In 2 to the power 3, the base is 2 and the exponent is 3.
Calculate: 2 to the power 3 means 2 x 2 x 2 = 8.
Large numbers: Exponents write very large numbers in short form, like 10 to the power 5 = 1,00,000.

studyContent.title: Laws of Exponents
studyContent.intro: The laws of exponents are rules for multiplying, dividing and taking powers of numbers with exponents.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: There are simple rules, called the laws of exponents, that make working with exponents easier (for the same base). When multiplying powers of the same base, we add the exponents: a to the m times a to the n equals a to the (m + n). When dividing powers of the same base, we subtract the exponents: a to the m divided by a to the n equals a to the (m - n). When taking a power of a power, we multiply the exponents: (a to the m) to the n equals a to the (m x n). Also, any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1. These laws help simplify expressions with exponents quickly.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Laws of exponents (same base): multiplying adds exponents, dividing subtracts them, a power of a power multiplies them, and any number to the power 0 is 1.
stepByStep: Multiplying: For the same base, add the exponents: a^m x a^n = a^(m+n).
Dividing: For the same base, subtract the exponents: a^m / a^n = a^(m-n).
Power of a power: Multiply the exponents: (a^m)^n = a^(m x n).
Power zero: Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1.

keywords.meaning: Rules for operating on powers.
keywords.example: a^m x a^n = a^(m+n) is a law of exponents.

keywords.meaning: The number being raised to a power.
keywords.example: The laws apply for the same base.

keywords.meaning: The power a base is raised to.

studyContent.realLifeExample: 2 to the power 3 times 2 to the power 2 = 2 to the power 5 = 32.

studyContent.title: Standard Form
studyContent.intro: Standard form expresses very large numbers using powers of 10, making them easy to write and read.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Very large numbers are difficult to write and read in full, so we use the standard form (also called scientific notation). In standard form, a number is written as a number between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10. For example, 59,000 is written as 5.9 x 10 to the power 4, and the speed of light, 300,000,000 m/s, is written as 3 x 10 to the power 8. To write a number in standard form, we place the decimal point after the first digit and count how many places it moved to find the power of 10. Standard form is widely used in science to handle very large (and very small) numbers neatly.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Standard form writes big numbers as a number between 1 and 10 times a power of 10. So 59,000 = 5.9 x 10 to the power 4.
stepByStep: Why use it: Standard form makes very large numbers easy to write and read.
The form: A number between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10.
Write it: Place the decimal after the first digit and count the places moved for the power.
Example: 59,000 = 5.9 x 10 to the power 4; used widely in science.

studyContent.title: Line Symmetry
studyContent.intro: A figure has line symmetry if a line divides it into two identical mirror-image halves.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has line symmetry (or reflection symmetry) if there is a line that divides it into two parts that are mirror images of each other. This line is called the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry. If we fold the figure along this line, the two halves match exactly. A figure may have no line of symmetry, one, or many; for example, a rectangle has 2 lines of symmetry, an equilateral triangle has 3, a square has 4, and a circle has countless lines of symmetry. Many letters, shapes and objects in nature show line symmetry. Identifying lines of symmetry helps us understand balanced shapes.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A figure has line symmetry if a line splits it into two mirror-image halves that match when folded. A square has 4 lines of symmetry; a circle has countless.
stepByStep: What line symmetry is: A line divides the figure into two mirror-image halves.
Line of symmetry: This dividing line is the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry.
Test by folding: Folding along the line makes the two halves match exactly.
Number of lines: Rectangle has 2, equilateral triangle 3, square 4, circle countless.

keywords.meaning: A line dividing a figure into mirror-image halves.
keywords.example: A butterfly has a line of symmetry.

studyContent.title: Rotational Symmetry
studyContent.intro: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after being turned by less than a full turn about a centre.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if, when it is rotated (turned) about a fixed central point by some angle less than a full turn, it looks exactly the same as it did at the start. The fixed point is called the centre of rotation. The number of times the figure looks the same during one complete turn is called the order of rotational symmetry. For example, a square has rotational symmetry of order 4 because it looks the same after every quarter turn (90 degrees), and an equilateral triangle has order 3. Every figure looks the same after a full turn, but only figures with order 2 or more truly have rotational symmetry.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a partial turn about its centre. A square matches 4 times in a full turn (order 4).
stepByStep: Turn about a centre: Rotate the figure about a fixed point called the centre of rotation.
Looks the same: If it looks the same after a turn less than full, it has rotational symmetry.
Order of symmetry: The number of times it matches in one full turn is the order.
Examples: A square has order 4; an equilateral triangle has order 3.

keywords.meaning: Looking the same after a partial turn.
The fixed point a figure turns about.
keywords.example: The centre of a square is its centre of rotation.

keywords.meaning: The number of matches in a full turn.

studyContent.realLifeExample: A square looks the same after each 90-degree turn, so its order is 4.

studyContent.title: Plane Figures and Solid Shapes
studyContent.intro: Plane figures are two-dimensional (flat), while solid shapes are three-dimensional and occupy space.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Shapes are of two kinds. Plane figures are two-dimensional (2D); they have only length and breadth and lie flat, like squares, rectangles, triangles and circles. Solid shapes are three-dimensional (3D); they have length, breadth and height (or depth) and take up space, like cubes, cuboids, cylinders, cones, spheres and pyramids. Many everyday objects are solid shapes: a dice is a cube, a ball is a sphere, and a tin can is a cylinder. Understanding the difference between 2D and 3D shapes helps us describe and study the objects around us.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Plane figures are flat 2D shapes (square, circle); solid shapes are 3D and take up space (cube, sphere, cylinder). A dice is a cube, a ball is a sphere.
stepByStep: Plane figures: Two-dimensional flat shapes with only length and breadth, like squares and circles.
Solid shapes: Three-dimensional shapes with length, breadth and height, like cubes and spheres.
Everyday solids: A dice is a cube, a ball a sphere, a tin a cylinder.
Why it matters: Knowing 2D and 3D shapes helps us describe objects around us.

keywords.example: A circle is a plane figure.

studyContent.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices
studyContent.intro: Solid shapes are described by their faces (flat surfaces), edges (where faces meet) and vertices (corners).
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Every solid shape can be described by counting three things: its faces, edges and vertices. A face is a flat (or curved) surface of the solid, an edge is the line segment where two faces meet, and a vertex is a corner point where edges meet. For example, a cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, and a cuboid has the same counts. A cylinder has 2 flat faces and 1 curved face, while a cone has 1 flat face, 1 curved face and 1 vertex. Counting faces, edges and vertices helps us identify and compare different solid shapes.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Solids have faces (surfaces), edges (where faces meet) and vertices (corners). A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.
stepByStep: Face: A face is a flat or curved surface of a solid.
Edge: An edge is the line where two faces meet.
Vertex: A vertex is a corner point where edges meet.
Counting them: A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices; a cone has 1 vertex.

keywords.meaning: A surface of a solid shape.
The line where two faces meet.
A corner where edges meet.

studyContent.realLifeExample: A cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.

studyContent.title: Nets and Views of Solids
studyContent.intro: A net is a flat shape that folds into a solid, and solids can be viewed from the top, front and side.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: We can understand solid shapes in two helpful ways. A net is a flat (2D) shape that can be folded to make a solid (3D) shape; for example, the net of a cube is made of six squares joined together, and a cylinder's net is two circles and a rectangle. We can also look at a solid from different directions: the top view, the front view and the side view each show a different flat shape of the same solid. These ideas help us connect 2D and 3D shapes, picture solids from a drawing, and are useful in design, model-making and reading plans.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A net is a flat shape that folds into a solid (six squares fold into a cube). Solids also have top, front and side views.
stepByStep: What a net is: A net is a flat shape that folds into a solid shape.
Examples of nets: A cube's net is six squares; a cylinder's net is two circles and a rectangle.
Views of solids: We can look at a solid from the top, the front and the side.
Why it helps: Nets and views connect 2D and 3D shapes and help in design and plans.

studyContent.title: वन्दे भारतमातरम् (देशभक्ति गीत)
studyContent.intro: यह एक देशभक्ति से भरा संस्कृत गीत है जिसमें भारतमाता की वंदना (नमन) की गई है।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: यह पाठ एक सुंदर देशभक्तिपूर्ण संस्कृत गीत है जिसमें कवि भारतमाता को नमन करता है। 'वन्दे भारतमातरम्' का अर्थ है — 'मैं भारतमाता की वंदना करता हूँ'। इस गीत में भारत की नदियों, पर्वतों, खेतों, हरियाली तथा उसकी महान संस्कृति की प्रशंसा की गई है और मातृभूमि के प्रति प्रेम, गर्व और सम्मान का भाव व्यक्त किया गया है। यह पाठ विद्यार्थियों में देशप्रेम जगाता है और सरल संस्कृत के माध्यम से मातृभूमि की महिमा का गान करता है।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: 'वन्दे भारतमातरम्' एक देशभक्ति गीत है जिसमें भारतमाता को नमन किया गया है और भारत की नदियों, पर्वतों तथा संस्कृति की प्रशंसा की गई है।
stepByStep: भारतमाता की वंदना: इस गीत में कवि भारतमाता को नमन करता है।
अर्थ: 'वन्दे भारतमातरम्' का अर्थ है — मैं भारतमाता की वंदना करता हूँ।
भारत की प्रशंसा: गीत में भारत की नदियों, पर्वतों, हरियाली और संस्कृति की प्रशंसा है।
देशप्रेम का भाव: यह पाठ मातृभूमि के प्रति प्रेम, गर्व और सम्मान जगाता है।

studyContent.title: नित्यं पिबामः सुभाषितरसम् (सुभाषित)
studyContent.intro: यह पाठ सुभाषितों (नीति-वचनों) का संग्रह है जो जीवन की महत्वपूर्ण सीख सरल संस्कृत में देते हैं।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: इस पाठ का शीर्षक 'नित्यं पिबामः सुभाषितरसम्' का अर्थ है — 'हम प्रतिदिन सुभाषितों के रस का पान करें'। सुभाषित अच्छे और बुद्धिमानी भरे वचन होते हैं जो थोड़े शब्दों में जीवन की बड़ी सीख देते हैं। इस पाठ में परिश्रम, विद्या, सच्चाई, मित्रता और अच्छे आचरण जैसे विषयों पर सुंदर सुभाषित दिए गए हैं। इन्हें कंठस्थ करके और जीवन में उतारकर हम अच्छे गुण सीख सकते हैं। यह पाठ संस्कृत भाषा की सुंदरता और भारतीय नीति-ज्ञान का परिचय कराता है।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: 'सुभाषित' अच्छे और बुद्धिमानी भरे वचन हैं जो थोड़े शब्दों में बड़ी सीख देते हैं। यह पाठ परिश्रम, विद्या और सच्चाई जैसे सुभाषितों का संग्रह है।
stepByStep: सुभाषित का अर्थ: सुभाषित अच्छे और बुद्धिमानी भरे वचन होते हैं।
शीर्षक का भाव: 'नित्यं पिबामः सुभाषितरसम्' = हम प्रतिदिन सुभाषितों का रस पिएँ।
सीख देने वाले विषय: इनमें परिश्रम, विद्या, सच्चाई, मित्रता और अच्छे आचरण की सीख है।
कंठस्थ करना: इन्हें याद करके और जीवन में उतारकर हम अच्छे गुण सीखते हैं।

studyContent.title: मित्राय नमः (योग और सूर्य)
studyContent.intro: यह पाठ संवाद के रूप में योग, सूर्य नमस्कार और सूर्य (मित्र) के महत्व के बारे में है।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: इस पाठ में दो मित्रों के संवाद के माध्यम से योग और सूर्य नमस्कार का महत्व बताया गया है। एक पात्र प्रतिदिन प्रातः अपने पिता के साथ उद्यान (बगीचे) जाकर योगासन करता है। 'मित्राय नमः' सूर्य नमस्कार के मंत्रों में से एक है, जिसमें सूर्य को मित्र मानकर नमस्कार किया जाता है, क्योंकि सूर्य हमें प्रकाश, ऊर्जा और स्वास्थ्य देता है। पाठ में योगासन और सूर्य नमस्कार से शरीर तथा मन के स्वास्थ्य का महत्व समझाया गया है। यह पाठ योग और स्वस्थ जीवन-शैली की प्रेरणा देता है।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: इस पाठ में मित्रों के संवाद से योग और सूर्य नमस्कार का महत्व बताया गया है। 'मित्राय नमः' सूर्य को मित्र मानकर किया गया नमस्कार है।
stepByStep: मित्रों का संवाद: पाठ में दो मित्रों के संवाद से योग का महत्व बताया गया है।
प्रातः योग: एक पात्र प्रतिदिन प्रातः पिता के साथ उद्यान जाकर योगासन करता है।
सूर्य = मित्र: 'मित्राय नमः' में सूर्य को मित्र मानकर नमस्कार किया जाता है।
स्वास्थ्य का महत्व: योगासन और सूर्य नमस्कार से शरीर और मन का स्वास्थ्य अच्छा रहता है।

topicTitle: न लभ्यते चेत् आम्लं द्राक्षाफलम् (लोमड़ी और अंगूर की कथा)
sourceLabel: Based on NCERT दीपकम् + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: न लभ्यते चेत् आम्लं द्राक्षाफलम् (लोमड़ी और अंगूर की कथा)
studyContent.intro: यह लोमड़ी और खट्टे अंगूरों की प्रसिद्ध कथा है, जो 'अंगूर खट्टे हैं' वाली प्रवृत्ति को दर्शाती है।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: यह पाठ लोमड़ी (शृगाल) और अंगूरों (द्राक्षाफल) की प्रसिद्ध नीति-कथा है। एक भूखी लोमड़ी ऊँचाई पर लटके हुए अंगूरों को देखती है और उन्हें खाने के लिए बार-बार उछलती है, पर वह उन तक नहीं पहुँच पाती। अंत में, जब वह अंगूर नहीं पा सकती, तो यह कहकर चली जाती है कि 'ये अंगूर तो खट्टे हैं'। शीर्षक 'न लभ्यते चेत् आम्लं द्राक्षाफलम्' का अर्थ है — 'यदि न मिले तो अंगूर खट्टा है'। यह कथा उस मनोवृत्ति पर व्यंग्य करती है जिसमें कोई चीज़ न मिलने पर हम उसे बुरा बताने लगते हैं। यह हमें ईमानदारी और सच्चे प्रयास की सीख देती है।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: इस कथा में भूखी लोमड़ी ऊँचे अंगूरों तक नहीं पहुँच पाती और कहती है कि 'अंगूर खट्टे हैं'। यह 'अंगूर खट्टे हैं' वाली प्रवृत्ति को दर्शाती है।
stepByStep: भूखी लोमड़ी: एक भूखी लोमड़ी ऊँचाई पर लटके अंगूरों को देखती है।
बार-बार प्रयास: वह अंगूर खाने के लिए बार-बार उछलती है पर पहुँच नहीं पाती।
अंगूर खट्टे हैं: न पाने पर वह कहती है कि 'ये अंगूर तो खट्टे हैं'।
सीख: यह कथा किसी चीज़ को न पाने पर उसे बुरा बताने की प्रवृत्ति पर व्यंग्य करती है।

sourceLabel: Based on NCERT दीपकम् + AI-generated teacher-style explanation

studyContent.title: सेवा हि परमो धर्मः (सेवा का महत्व)
studyContent.intro: यह पाठ बताता है कि दूसरों की सेवा करना ही सबसे बड़ा धर्म (कर्तव्य) है।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: इस पाठ का शीर्षक 'सेवा हि परमो धर्मः' का अर्थ है — 'सेवा ही सबसे बड़ा धर्म है'। इस पाठ में किसी कथा या उदाहरण के माध्यम से यह सिखाया गया है कि दूसरों की निःस्वार्थ भाव से सेवा करना सबसे महान कार्य है। बीमारों, बुज़ुर्गों, ज़रूरतमंदों और पशु-पक्षियों की सेवा करने से हमें सच्चा सुख और संतोष मिलता है, और समाज भी अच्छा बनता है। पाठ यह संदेश देता है कि हमें दूसरों की सहायता और सेवा के लिए सदा तैयार रहना चाहिए, क्योंकि सेवा ही मनुष्य का सबसे ऊँचा कर्तव्य है।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: 'सेवा हि परमो धर्मः' का अर्थ है — सेवा ही सबसे बड़ा धर्म है। दूसरों की निःस्वार्थ सेवा करना सबसे महान कार्य है और इससे सच्चा सुख मिलता है।
stepByStep: शीर्षक का अर्थ: 'सेवा हि परमो धर्मः' = सेवा ही सबसे बड़ा धर्म है।
निःस्वार्थ सेवा: दूसरों की बिना स्वार्थ के सेवा करना सबसे महान कार्य है।
किसकी सेवा: बीमारों, बुज़ुर्गों, ज़रूरतमंदों और पशु-पक्षियों की सेवा करनी चाहिए।
सच्चा सुख: सेवा करने से सच्चा सुख और संतोष मिलता है और समाज अच्छा बनता है।

सबसे बड़ा, सर्वोच्च।
कर्तव्य।

studyContent.title: क्रीडामः वयं श्लोकान्त्याक्षरीम् (श्लोक अंत्याक्षरी)
studyContent.intro: यह पाठ श्लोक-अंत्याक्षरी नामक खेल के माध्यम से संस्कृत के सुंदर श्लोकों का परिचय कराता है।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: इस पाठ का शीर्षक 'क्रीडामः वयं श्लोकान्त्याक्षरीम्' का अर्थ है — 'आओ, हम श्लोक-अंत्याक्षरी खेलें'। अंत्याक्षरी एक मनोरंजक खेल है जिसमें एक श्लोक के अंतिम अक्षर से अगला श्लोक आरंभ किया जाता है। इस खेल के माध्यम से पाठ में अनेक सुंदर और सरल संस्कृत श्लोक दिए गए हैं, जो जीवन की सीख देते हैं। यह खेल विद्यार्थियों को खेल-खेल में श्लोक कंठस्थ करने, संस्कृत भाषा से प्रेम करने और एक साथ मिलकर सीखने के लिए प्रेरित करता है। इस प्रकार यह पाठ ज्ञान और आनंद दोनों देता है।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: 'श्लोक-अंत्याक्षरी' एक खेल है जिसमें श्लोक के अंतिम अक्षर से अगला श्लोक शुरू होता है। यह पाठ खेल-खेल में सुंदर संस्कृत श्लोक सिखाता है।
stepByStep: शीर्षक का अर्थ: 'क्रीडामः वयं श्लोकान्त्याक्षरीम्' = आओ, हम श्लोक-अंत्याक्षरी खेलें।
अंत्याक्षरी का खेल: इसमें एक श्लोक के अंतिम अक्षर से अगला श्लोक आरंभ होता है।
सुंदर श्लोक: खेल के माध्यम से अनेक सरल और सुंदर श्लोक दिए गए हैं।
ज्ञान और आनंद: यह खेल-खेल में श्लोक कंठस्थ करने और संस्कृत से प्रेम करने की प्रेरणा देता है।

keywords.meaning: हम खेलते हैं।
पद्य; नीति या स्तुति की पंक्ति।
अंतिम अक्षर से शुरू करने का खेल।

studyContent.realLifeExample: अंत्याक्षरी में श्लोक के अंतिम अक्षर से अगला श्लोक शुरू होता है।

studyContent.title: ईशावास्यम् इदं सर्वम् (उपनिषद् का संदेश)
studyContent.intro: यह पाठ ईशावास्य उपनिषद् के प्रसिद्ध श्लोक पर आधारित है, जो बताता है कि यह सारा संसार ईश्वर से व्याप्त है।
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: इस पाठ का शीर्षक 'ईशावास्यम् इदं सर्वम्' ईशावास्य उपनिषद् के एक प्रसिद्ध मंत्र से लिया गया है, जिसका अर्थ है — 'यह सारा संसार ईश्वर से व्याप्त है'। इसका भाव यह है कि इस जगत में जो कुछ भी है, उसमें ईश्वर का वास है, इसलिए हमें किसी भी वस्तु पर लालच नहीं करना चाहिए और जो हमें मिला है उसी में संतोष तथा त्याग की भावना रखनी चाहिए। पाठ यह गहरा भारतीय आध्यात्मिक विचार सरल रूप में समझाता है कि पूरी सृष्टि एक है और सब में एक ही ईश्वर है, अतः हमें सब प्राणियों के प्रति प्रेम और आदर रखना चाहिए।
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: 'ईशावास्यम् इदं सर्वम्' का अर्थ है — यह सारा संसार ईश्वर से व्याप्त है। इसलिए हमें लालच नहीं, बल्कि संतोष और त्याग की भावना रखनी चाहिए।
stepByStep: उपनिषद् का मंत्र: यह पाठ ईशावास्य उपनिषद् के प्रसिद्ध मंत्र पर आधारित है।
अर्थ: 'ईशावास्यम् इदं सर्वम्' = यह सारा संसार ईश्वर से व्याप्त है।
संतोष और त्याग: सब में ईश्वर है, इसलिए लालच न करके संतोष और त्याग रखना चाहिए।
सृष्टि एक है: पूरी सृष्टि एक है; अतः सब प्राणियों से प्रेम और आदर रखना चाहिए।

keywords.meaning: ईश्वर।
सब कुछ।
जो मिला है उसमें खुश रहना।

studyContent.realLifeExample: 'ईशावास्यम् इदं सर्वम्' का अर्थ है — सब कुछ ईश्वर से व्याप्त है।
